---
title: "Bayesian Time Series and State-Space Models: Dynamics, Filtering, and Forecasting"
---

## Learning Outcomes

After this chapter, you should be able to:

- write dynamic linear models as Bayesian state-space factorizations;
- derive the posterior update in an AR(1) Gaussian model conceptually;
- explain local level, local trend, and stochastic-volatility style structure;
- distinguish filtering, smoothing, and forecasting;
- connect state-space modeling to Kalman and particle methods.

## 1. Dynamic Bayesian Modeling

Static models assume one latent parameter vector explains all observations. Time series models relax this by allowing latent states to evolve:

$$
x_t \sim p(x_t \mid x_{t-1},\theta),
\qquad
y_t \sim p(y_t \mid x_t,\theta).
$$

This structure captures persistence, shocks, seasonal dynamics, and regime changes.

## 2. Dynamic Linear Models

A general dynamic linear model writes

$$
x_t = G_t x_{t-1} + w_t,\qquad w_t \sim \mathcal{N}(0,W_t),
$$

$$
y_t = F_t^\top x_t + v_t,\qquad v_t \sim \mathcal{N}(0,V_t).
$$

When $(G_t,F_t,W_t,V_t)$ are known, inference for the latent states is linear Gaussian and therefore tractable through Kalman filtering and smoothing.

## 3. Local Level Model

The simplest state-space model is

$$
x_t = x_{t-1} + w_t,\qquad w_t \sim \mathcal{N}(0,W),
$$

$$
y_t = x_t + v_t,\qquad v_t \sim \mathcal{N}(0,V).
$$

This model expresses a slowly evolving latent mean observed with noise.

### Interpretation

- $W$ controls state volatility;
- $V$ controls measurement noise;
- the ratio $W/V$ determines how rapidly the latent signal adapts to new data.

## 4. Bayesian AR(1) Thinking

An AR(1) model can be written as

$$
y_t = \phi y_{t-1} + \varepsilon_t,\qquad \varepsilon_t \sim \mathcal{N}(0,\sigma^2),
$$

or more explicitly in Bayesian regression form by conditioning on lagged responses:

$$
y_{2:T} \mid \phi,\sigma^2 \sim \mathcal{N}(X\phi,\sigma^2 I),
$$

where $X=(y_1,\dots,y_{T-1})^\top$.

Thus Bayesian AR models can often be analyzed using the same regression logic developed earlier, though stationarity priors on $\phi$ may need to restrict $|\phi|<1$.

## 5. Forecasting

Bayesian forecasting requires integrating over state and parameter uncertainty. For a one-step forecast,

$$
p(y_{t+1} \mid y_{1:t})
=
\int p(y_{t+1} \mid x_{t+1},\theta)\,
p(x_{t+1},\theta \mid y_{1:t})\,
dx_{t+1}\, d\theta.
$$

This is more honest than plug-in forecasting because it propagates uncertainty in dynamics and parameters forward in time.

## 6. Smoothing

Filtering targets

$$
p(x_t \mid y_{1:t}),
$$

while smoothing targets

$$
p(x_t \mid y_{1:T}).
$$

Smoothing uses future observations as well as past observations to infer the latent state at time $t$, so it is always at least as informed as filtering when applied retrospectively.

## 7. Hidden Markov Models And Regime Switching

When the latent state is discrete, one obtains a hidden Markov model. This is useful when the dynamics are better described by regimes than by continuous latent trajectories.

Examples:

- bull versus bear market states;
- animal behavioral modes;
- low versus high volatility regimes.

The Bayesian issues then include transition probabilities, emission parameters, label switching, and state uncertainty.

## 8. Stochastic Volatility

A simple stochastic volatility model writes

$$
y_t \mid h_t \sim \mathcal{N}(0,\exp(h_t)),
$$

$$
h_t = \mu + \phi(h_{t-1}-\mu)+\eta_t,\qquad \eta_t \sim \mathcal{N}(0,\sigma_h^2).
$$

This is a state-space model with nonlinear observation variance. It illustrates why particle methods and HMC-based latent-state inference become necessary beyond the Gaussian linear case.

## 9. `R` Workflow

The local-level particle-filter example is in [particle_filter_local_level.R](../../labs/r/particle_filter_local_level.R). The HMM recursion lab is in [hmm_forward_algorithm.R](../../labs/r/hmm_forward_algorithm.R).

These labs complement the filtering chapter by showing how model structure and computation fit together in dynamic settings.

## 10. Diagnostics And Pitfalls

### Confusing observation noise and state noise

These play distinct inferential roles and should not be conflated.

### Overfitting dynamic variation

Allowing very large state variance can make the latent state chase noise.

### Ignoring forecast calibration

Good in-sample fit does not guarantee good predictive performance.

### Forgetting initial-state assumptions

Short series can be sensitive to the prior or distribution assumed for $x_0$.

## 11. Exercises

1. Write the local-level model as a state-space factorization and identify all conditional independences.
2. Explain why AR(1) regression can be viewed as a special case of Bayesian regression with lagged predictors.
3. Distinguish filtering, smoothing, and forecasting both mathematically and substantively.
4. Describe how stochastic volatility differs from a local-level Gaussian model.
5. Explain why hidden-state models are natural for regime-switching phenomena.

## 12. Summary

Bayesian time-series modeling extends the regression worldview into dynamic latent structure:

- states evolve;
- observations are conditionally independent given states;
- forecasting, filtering, and smoothing become distinct inferential tasks;
- the computational strategy depends strongly on whether the model is linear Gaussian, discrete-state, or nonlinear.

## References

Key references are @gelman2013, @doucet2001, @delmoral2004, and @murphy2023.
